refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total operational time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement for remote equipment control. Refinement clarifies the measurement basis (bidirectional link), acceptance threshold (≥0.999), and evaluation scope (agreed period). High safety criticality demands explicit availability targets to prevent loss-of-control scenarios.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) with timestamped logs; calculation of availability as (total_uptime / total_evaluation_period); comparison against 0.999 threshold; root-cause analysis of any downtime events.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain network redundancy to achieve ≥0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics to the Site Manager.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement dual independent network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log all state transitions.
DP-B: Implement single primary network path with periodic health checks and manual failover capability.
DP-C: Implement triple-redundant network paths with voting logic and automatic failover; continuous health monitoring with predictive diagnostics.
DP-D: Implement primary network path with secondary satellite link; automatic switchover on primary failure; continuous telemetry validation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement dual independent network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log all state transitions.

potential_sources: Link health monitoring logic couples to telemetry collection (Telemetry interface dependency).
Failover logic couples to vehicle control (Tasking interface dependency).
Availability logging couples to safety alerting (SafetyAlert interface dependency on link loss events).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable and expected at this level; they represent necessary information flows. No circular dependencies detected. DP-A balances cost, complexity, and achievability of 0.999 target without over-engineering (DP-C) or under-engineering (DP-B).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Two independent network paths (e.g., cellular + private radio) are available at the worksite.
Assumption: Automatic failover logic can be implemented with latency <100ms to prevent safety-critical command loss.
Risk: If both paths fail simultaneously, availability target cannot be met; mitigation requires geographic/technology diversity.
Risk: Failover logic complexity may introduce new failure modes; requires rigorous testing and validation.
Risk: Continuous health monitoring increases power consumption and computational load on vehicle platform.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly specify: (1) network path independence criteria (geographic, technology, operator), (2) failover latency budget, (3) health check frequency and timeout thresholds, (4) logging granularity and retention policy.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-path network architecture, failover logic, link health monitoring, and availability logging. Interfaces with Vehicle for command injection and telemetry collection; interfaces with Safety Services for link-loss alerting.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation sends operator commands to Vehicle via Tasking interface; failover logic ensures commands are routed through active path.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation receives vehicle state and sensor data from Vehicle via Telemetry interface; used for link health validation and operator situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation sends link-loss alerts to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface to trigger safe state transitions and operator notifications.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation manages dual Network Link instances (primary and backup); monitors link state, initiates failover, and logs availability metrics.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover mechanisms, and network path management. High containment; minimal diffusion. This is the primary responsibility domain.

Safety Services owns safe state transitions and operator alerting, but NOT the network redundancy or failover logic. Link availability is a Teleoperation concern; Safety Services reacts to link-loss events.
Vehicle executes commands and provides telemetry, but does not own link availability. Vehicle may implement local failsafe logic, but network path management belongs in Teleoperation.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; link availability is a sub-system concern owned by Teleoperation module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns all dual-path network logic, failover state machines, health monitoring, and availability metrics. Vehicle module provides command/telemetry interfaces. Safety Services module consumes SafetyAlert notifications to trigger safe state transitions. This allocation minimizes diffusion and aligns with functional responsibility.
containment_risks: Risk: If failover logic is split between Teleoperation and Vehicle (e.g., Vehicle implements local timeout), inconsistent behavior may result. Mitigation: Centralize all failover logic in Teleoperation; Vehicle is passive responder.

Risk: If health monitoring is duplicated across Teleoperation and Safety Services, divergent link-state views may cause safety violations. Mitigation: Single source of truth in Teleoperation; Safety Services subscribes to SafetyAlert notifications.

Risk: If network path selection logic is embedded in multiple code locations, maintenance and testing become difficult. Mitigation: Encapsulate path selection in a single Teleoperation module component.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define Teleoperation module interfaces: (1) Tasking output (command routing), (2) Telemetry input (health validation), (3) SafetyAlert output (link-loss notification), (4) Network Link inout (dual-path management). Document that Vehicle and Safety Services are consumers, not contributors, to link availability logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (dual-path with automatic failover) directly addresses FR requirement for ≥0.999 availability. DP logic is traceable to FR acceptance criteria. No ambiguity in mapping. DP is implementable and testable.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Failover logic may require coordination with Vehicle module (command routing). Mitigation: Define clear Tasking interface contract; Teleoperation owns routing decision.
Moderate risk: Health monitoring may require Vehicle telemetry validation. Mitigation: Telemetry interface provides raw data; Teleoperation owns validation logic.
Low risk: Safety alerting requires SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services. Mitigation: Interface is well-defined; no diffusion expected.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. Dual-path network architecture, failover state machines, and availability logging are naturally contained within Teleoperation. External dependencies (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are interface-based and well-scoped. No significant diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Availability can be measured directly via link state logs. Failover behavior can be tested via network fault injection. Metrics can be collected and reported to Site Manager. Verification is feasible within agreed evaluation period.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Teleoperation owns link availability; Vehicle and Safety Services have defined, limited roles. Ownership boundaries are explicit. Interfaces are named and directional.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-A to specify failover latency budget (e.g., <100ms) and health check frequency (e.g., every 1 second).
Define Teleoperation module interface contracts in detail: Tasking (command format, routing logic), Telemetry (validation criteria), SafetyAlert (alert types and timing).
Establish acceptance test plan: dual-path availability measurement, failover latency verification, simultaneous-failure scenario testing.
Document assumptions about network path independence and availability at worksite.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs addressing link detection, safe state transition, network redundancy, and metrics reporting. Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. DP-A is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module, but sub-level decomposition is necessary to detail failover logic, health monitoring, and safety integration. Proceeding to next level of functional decomposition is appropriate.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain network redundancy to achieve ≥0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log and report link availability metrics to the Site Manager.

assumptions: Two independent network paths (e.g., cellular + private radio) are available at the worksite.
Automatic failover logic can be implemented with latency <100ms.
Vehicle platform can support dual-path command routing without performance degradation.
Safety Services module is capable of receiving and acting on SafetyAlert notifications within safety-critical timing constraints.
Agreed evaluation period is sufficient to collect statistically significant availability data (e.g., 30 days minimum).
open_risks: Network path independence may not be achievable at all worksites; customer acceptance criteria may need to be tailored per site.
Failover latency budget may conflict with other real-time constraints in Vehicle or Teleoperation modules.
Simultaneous failure of both network paths is a low-probability, high-consequence event; mitigation strategy (e.g., safe state hold, operator notification) must be defined.
Continuous health monitoring increases power and computational load; impact on Vehicle battery life and Teleoperation processing capacity must be quantified.
Availability logging may generate large data volumes; storage and transmission strategy must be defined.
evidence_needed: Network path availability data from candidate worksites (cellular coverage, private radio range, interference patterns).
Failover latency measurements from prototype implementation.
Safety analysis of link-loss scenarios and safe state transition requirements.
Power consumption and computational load estimates for dual-path monitoring.
Customer acceptance criteria and evaluation period definition.
Test plan for failover behavior, simultaneous-failure scenarios, and availability measurement.
